title: About
text:
Hi! I am Cassie Nesheim, a junior at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln majoring in Data Science with a minor in Sociology. I am passionate about using data to uncover insights, tell meaningful stories, and support better decision-making, especially in the world of sports.
I have experience working with Python and R to clean, analyze, and visualize data, along with a strong foundation in statistics. My analysis work goes beyond the basics, incorporating methods like hypothesis testing and other statistical techniques to draw deeper, more reliable, confident conclusions.
As a former gymnast, I bring a unique perspective to sports analytics, particularly in gymnastics. This blog features quite a bit of gymnastics analysis, along with a variety of other sports. View my posts to see some of my work!

title: Using GDP to Discover which Countries Under and Over Performed at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggalt)
library(gt)
library(treemapify)
library(ggalt)

event_medals <- read_csv("data/medals.csv")
medal_totals <- read_csv("data/medals_total.csv")

gdp_pop <- read_csv("data/gdp_pop.csv") |>
  rename (
    year = Time,
    country_name = `Country Name`,
    country_code = `Country Code`,
    gdp_current_usd = `GDP (current US$) [NY.GDP.MKTP.CD]`,
    gdp_per_capita = `GDP per capita (current US$) [NY.GDP.PCAP.CD]`,
    population = `Population, total [SP.POP.TOTL]`,
    gdp_growth_annpct = `GDP growth (annual %) [NY.GDP.MKTP.KD.ZG]`
  ) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_current_usd = na_if(gdp_current_usd, ".."),
    gdp_per_capita = na_if(gdp_per_capita, ".."),
    population = na_if(population, ".."),
    gdp_growth_annpct = na_if(gdp_growth_annpct, "..")
  ) |>
  select(year, country_code, gdp_current_usd, gdp_per_capita, population, gdp_growth_annpct) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_current_usd = as.numeric(gdp_current_usd),
    gdp_per_capita = as.numeric(gdp_per_capita),
    population = as.numeric(population),
    gdp_growth_annpct = as.numeric(gdp_growth_annpct)
  ) |>
  add_row(
    country_code = "TPE",
    gdp_current_usd = 797000000000,
    gdp_per_capita = 34060,
    population = 23400000,
    gdp_growth_annpct = 4.8
  ) |>
  add_row(
    country_code = "PRK",
    gdp_current_usd = NA,
    gdp_per_capita = NA,
    population = 26498823,
    gdp_growth_annpct = NA
  )

medal_gdp_pop_data <- left_join(medal_totals, gdp_pop, by="country_code") 

event_medals <- event_medals |>
  mutate(discipline = recode(discipline, "Athletics" = "Track & Field"))

medal_gdp_pop_scaled <- medal_gdp_pop_data |>
  mutate(
    tril_gdp = gdp_current_usd / 1000000000000,
    mil_pop = population / 1000000
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(tril_gdp)) |>
  mutate(country = recode(country, "Türkiye" = "Turkey"))


Most everyone knows that the United States tends to dominate medal count at each Summer Olympics, usually placing within the top three countries, if not at the very top. This includes leading in gold medals earned and overall total medals earned. China also consistently ranks among the top countries. What do these countries have in common? Massive GDP (gross domestic product). This means these countries have large economies, and, in theory, more resources to be able to compete well in Olympic games.
Looking at total medals compared to GDP, it is clear that the U.S. and China are in a league of their own. All the other countries end up in a small corner in the bottom left of the graph, signifying that the U.S. and China have such large GDPs and total medals earned that they overshadow the others. So, it is difficult to see how any other countries performed at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games compared to their GDP.


Code
medals_gdp_plot <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(data=medal_gdp_pop_scaled, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, size=mil_pop)) +
  geom_text_repel(data=medal_gdp_pop_scaled|>filter((tril_gdp>15)), aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, label=country)) +
  labs(title="U.S. and China own the rest of the world in GDP and Olympic medals.", 
       subtitle="With over $15 Trillion in GDP, the top two economies drown out the rest of the world into a small corner.", 
       y= "Total Olympic Medals Earned", 
       x = "GDP (Gross Domestic Product) in Trillions ($)",
    caption="Source: World Bank Group and Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim",
    size = "Population \n(in millions)"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    legend.title = element_text(size=10),
    legend.text = element_text(size=9),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

medals_gdp_plot









While it is difficult to see any trends in the other countries, there is some evidence of a linear relationship between GDP and total medals earned at the Olympics.
When China and the U.S. are removed from the dataset, this linear trend becomes even more noticeable. This trend reveals that having a higher GDP generally means countries will earn more medals at the Olympics. Using this trend, a linear regression line (the blue line) reveals how many medals each country is expected to earn at the Olympics given their GDP.


Code
medal_gdp_pop_filtered <- medal_gdp_pop_scaled |>
  filter(tril_gdp < 7) 

gdp_filtered_model <- lm(Total ~ tril_gdp, data=medal_gdp_pop_filtered)

medal_gdp_pop_filtered <- medal_gdp_pop_filtered |>
  mutate(resids = residuals(gdp_filtered_model), fitted = fitted(gdp_filtered_model)) |>
  arrange(desc(resids))

top3_countries <- medal_gdp_pop_filtered |> slice_max(order_by=resids, n=3)
bottom3_countries <- medal_gdp_pop_filtered |> slice_min(order_by=resids, n=3)
labeled_countries <- bind_rows(top3_countries, bottom3_countries)

medals_gdp_no_outliers <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(data=medal_gdp_pop_filtered, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, size=mil_pop)) +
  geom_point(data=top3_countries, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, size=mil_pop), color="#57C4AD") +
  geom_point(data=bottom3_countries, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, size=mil_pop), color="#DB4235") +
  geom_smooth(data=medal_gdp_pop_filtered, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total), method="lm", se=FALSE) +
  geom_text_repel(data=labeled_countries, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, label=country)) +
  guides(
    size = guide_legend(override.aes = list(color = "black"))
    ) +
  labs(title="Some countries capitalized at Paris, some failed to meet their mark.", 
       subtitle="Australia, France, and Great Britain earned far more medals than their GDP predicted, \nwhile Turkey, Mexico, and India left medals on the table.", 
       y= "Total Olympic Medals Earned", 
       x = "GDP (Gross Domestic Product) in Trillions ($)",
    caption="Source: World Bank Group and Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim",
    size = "Population \n(in millions)"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title = element_text(size=9),
    legend.text = element_text(size=8),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

medals_gdp_no_outliers









Looking at this graph, two things are clear. One, GDP and total medals earned are related. Two, some countries were able to capitalize on their resources and wildly outperform expectations. While others failed to utilize their resources to their potential, earning significantly fewer medals than expected.
When the U.S. and China are removed, India, Mexico, and Turkey emerge as the biggest losers in Paris. In other words, they performed the worst compared to how many medals their GDP predicted them to earn. They failed to turn their GDP and resources into medals on the biggest stage. Something to note is that India had the biggest population of these countries, not only were they unable to capitalize on GDP, they were also unable to capitalize on their larger population.
On the other hand, Australia, France, and Great Britain were able to take their resources and explode. They earned significantly more medals than their GDP predicted they would earn. One thing to note, since France was the host country, they had automatic bids in some disciplines that they might not traditionally qualify athletes in. In other words, they had more opportunities to medal than they normally might.
While this model does reduce the data and exclude the extreme outliers like the U.S. and China, it is clear there are still several countries that are below $1 trillion GDP that are being squished to the sidelines. By further analyzing smaller economies, we can see which countries were able to do more with less.


Code
medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril <- medal_gdp_pop_filtered |>
  filter(tril_gdp < 1)

gdp_less_1_tril_model <- lm(Total ~ tril_gdp, data=medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril)

medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril <- medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril |>
  mutate(resids_less1 = residuals(gdp_less_1_tril_model), fitted_less1 = fitted(gdp_less_1_tril_model))

top3_less1_countries <- medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril |> slice_max(order_by=resids_less1, n=3)
bottom3_less1_countries <- medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril |> slice_min(order_by=resids_less1, n=4)
labeled_countries_less1 <- bind_rows(top3_less1_countries, bottom3_less1_countries)

medals_gdp_less_1_tril <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(data=medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, size=mil_pop)) +
  geom_point(data=top3_less1_countries, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, size=mil_pop), color="#57C4AD") +
  geom_point(data=bottom3_less1_countries, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, size=mil_pop), color="#DB4235") +
  geom_smooth(data=medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total), method="lm", se=FALSE) +
  geom_text_repel(data=labeled_countries_less1, aes(x=tril_gdp, y=Total, label=country)) +
  guides(
    size = guide_legend(override.aes = list(color = "black"))
    ) +
  labs(title="New Zealand did the most with the least.", 
       subtitle="Of the countries with lower economies, New Zealand overperformed the most, \nwhile Singapore underperformed the most.", 
       y= "Total Olympic Medals Earned", 
       x = "GDP (Gross Domestic Product) in Trillions ($)",
    caption="Source: World Bank Group and Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim",
    size = "Population \n(in millions)"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title = element_text(size=9),
    legend.text = element_text(size=8),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

medals_gdp_less_1_tril









Analyzing only the countries with a GDP under $1 trillion and predicting their total medals at the Olympics based on GDP, we find that New Zealand, Hungary, and Uzbekistan were the lower-GDP countries that capitalized on their resources most. While Peru, Pakistan, Singapore, and Argentina were the countries that missed their mark most severely.
Interestingly, these three overperformers have relatively small populations, at least in comparison to the other lower-GDP countries. On the contrary, Pakistan was one of the lower economies who underperformed most significantly at the Paris Games, and it had the largest population of these countries. So, not only was Pakistan unable to turn their GDP into medals, they were also unable to capitalize on their larger population, similar to India above.
What about other countries with lower GDP? How do other countries stack up? And how far apart were the medals each country was expected to earn compared to their actual medal output?
Looking at this graph, we can see that of the 15 countries who under or overperformed most significantly among lower-GDP economies, only 6 countries overperformed expectations. Hungary and New Zealand jump out, as they overperformed and earned significantly more medals in Paris than their GDP predicted. The other countries either overperformed expectations less significantly or they underperformed and earned fewer medals than they were expected to based on GDP.


Code
dumbbell_data <- medal_gdp_pop_less_1_tril |>
  slice_max(order_by=abs(resids_less1), n=15)

dumbbell_plot <- ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=dumbbell_data, aes(y=reorder(country, Total), yend=country, x=fitted_less1, xend=Total), color="grey", linewidth = 1
  )  + 
  geom_point(
    data=dumbbell_data, aes(y=reorder(country, Total), x=fitted_less1, color="Expected"), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=dumbbell_data, aes(y=reorder(country, Total), x=Total, color="Actual"), size=2
  ) + 
  scale_color_manual(name="", values=c("Expected"="steelblue", "Actual"="magenta3")) +
  annotate("segment", x=0, xend=20.5, y=9.5, yend=9.5, linetype="solid", color="black", linewidth=0.5) +
  annotate("text", x=17, y=10, label="Overperformed", hjust=0, size=3, color="black") +
  annotate("text", x=17, y=9, label="Underperformed", hjust=0, size=3, color="black") +
  labs(title="Hungary and New Zealand made the most of limited resources.", 
       subtitle="Among countries with under $1 trillion in GDP, the gap between expected medals (blue dot) and \nactual medals (purple dot) shows how much each country over or underperformed their GDP prediction.*", 
       y = "",
       x = "Total Medals Earned",
    caption="*Note: Only the 15 countries with the largest gap between expected and actual are shown. \nSource: World Bank Group and Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, face = "plain"),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

dumbbell_plot









Now, let’s zoom back out and discover which countries underperformed and overperformed according to their GDP at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games when considering all countries.
After fitting all of the countries to a model, we find that France overperformed the most, earning about 42 more medals than they should have according to their GDP. And, on the other end, India underperformed the most, earning about 19 fewer medals than they should have according to their GDP.
Notably, though, when the United States is included, they come out as an UNDERperforming country. Yes, that’s right. Even though the U.S. came out on top and earned the most medals across all countries in Paris, according to GDP, they should have earned about 13 more medals than they did. However, because the U.S. GDP is so high, and their medal output is also so high, the expected medals earned is significantly higher than any other country. According to GDP, the U.S. should have earned 139 medals at the Paris Games. 139 medals would have put them even further ahead of all other countries than they already were. So, while the United States technically underperformed, since they are already so far ahead of all other countries, this finding is less impactful than the other countries who underperformed and overperformed.


Code
gdp_pop_model <- lm(Total ~ tril_gdp, data=medal_gdp_pop_scaled)

gdp_pop_full <- medal_gdp_pop_scaled |>
  mutate(resids_full = residuals(gdp_pop_model), fitted_full = fitted(gdp_pop_model)) |>
  arrange(desc(resids_full))

top5_countries_overall <- gdp_pop_full |> slice_max(order_by=resids_full, n=5)
bottom5_countries_overall <- gdp_pop_full |> slice_min(order_by=resids_full, n=5)
labeled_countries <- bind_rows(top5_countries_overall, bottom5_countries_overall) |>
  select(country, resids_full) |>
  arrange(desc(resids_full))

labeled_countries |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    country = "Country",
    resids_full = "Medals Away From Expected"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "The United States actually underperformed at Paris 2024 according to GDP.",
    subtitle = "Top 5 overperformers (green) and bottom 5 underperformers (red) by medals earned vs. GDP prediction."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Source: World Bank Group and Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_text(color = "red3")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      columns = resids_full,
      rows = resids_full < 0)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_text(color = "forestgreen")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      columns = resids_full,
      rows = resids_full > 0)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#bde8df")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = resids_full == max(resids_full))
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "#f3bfba")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = resids_full == min(resids_full))
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = resids_full,
    decimals = 1
  )








The United States actually underperformed at Paris 2024 according to GDP.


Top 5 overperformers (green) and bottom 5 underperformers (red) by medals earned vs. GDP prediction.


Country
Medals Away From Expected




France
42.4


Great Britain
41.0


Australia
37.9


Italy
22.0


Japan
19.5


Pakistan
−7.8


Singapore
−8.6


Mexico
−10.6


United States
−13.0


India
−19.0



Source: World Bank Group and Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim








So, we have seen that GDP is an indicator of how well countries will do at the Games. While some countries are able to use their resources to their full potential, and even earn more medals than they are “supposed to”, others fail to rise to the occasion and perform worse than they should.
But what makes overperforming countries succeed? Do countries who overperform tend to rely on one or two disciplines (sports) to do so? Or do countries who underperform do so because they specialize in only one or two disciplines and fail to compete in other disciplines?
An analysis of which disciplines the overperforming countries medaled in revealed that, aside from Australia and Hungary, many countries used breadth to their advantage and medaled across many disciplines.


Code
best_overperformers <- c("Australia", "France", "Great Britain", "Italy", "Japan", "New Zealand", "Hungary", "Uzbekistan")

best_events <- event_medals |>
  filter(country %in% best_overperformers) |>
  group_by(country, discipline) |>
  mutate(dis_count = n()) |>
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(discipline = ifelse(dis_count < max(dis_count)*0.10, "Other", discipline)) |>
  group_by(country, discipline) |>
  summarise(country_dis_count = sum(dis_count))

best_events <- best_events |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(
    proportion = country_dis_count/sum(country_dis_count),
    country_max_prop = max(proportion)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    percentage = paste0(round(proportion*100, 2), "%"),
    label = paste(discipline, percentage, sep = "\n"),
    country = reorder(country, -country_max_prop)
  )

best_treemap <- ggplot(best_events, aes(area=country_dis_count, fill=proportion, label=label)) +
  geom_treemap(color="black", linewidth=0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~country) +
  geom_treemap_text(color="gray32", place="center") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="#CFEFE8", high="#57C4AD") +
  labs(title="Some countries who overperform use breadth to their advantage, \nsome leverage specialties.", 
       subtitle="Most countries that punch above their GDP weight earn their medals across many disciplines. \nAustralia and Hungary are the exception, with each relying heavily on one sport.", 
       caption="Source: Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title = element_text(size=9),
    legend.text = element_text(size=8),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

best_treemap









While Australia relied on Swimming and Hungary relied on Canoe Sprint, all the other overperforming teams were able to do so by competing and medaling in a variety of disciplines. This breadth demonstrates how important it is for countries to be able to use their resources to diversify and provide ample opportunities to their people to train and compete in many, many disciplines. While not every country can outperform expectations, those countries who are able to qualify and compete athletes across disciplines seem to do better than their GDP expects them to.
Now, does this principle hold for underperforming teams? In theory, teams who underperformed expectations did so because they were unable to take advantage of resources. If the idea that breadth is a key to success is true, then those teams who underperformed should lack diversity in the disciplines they medaled in.
One thing to note, if a country only took home one medal, their breadth is obviously zero as their only medal could have only come from one discipline. So, these countries were excluded from breadth analysis. Let’s examine the breadth of the underperforming teams who took home at least two medals.


Code
event_medals <- event_medals |>
  mutate(country = recode(country, "Türkiye" = "Turkey"))

worst_underperformers <- c("India", "Mexico", "Turkey", "Argentina", "United States")

worst_events <- event_medals |>
  filter(country %in% worst_underperformers) |>
  group_by(country, discipline) |>
  mutate(dis_count = n()) |>
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(discipline = ifelse(dis_count < max(dis_count)*0.10, "Other", discipline)) |>
  group_by(country, discipline) |>
  summarise(country_dis_count = sum(dis_count))

worst_events <- worst_events |>
  group_by(country) |>
  mutate(proportion = country_dis_count/sum(country_dis_count),
         scaled_fill = proportion/sum(country_dis_count),
         country_max_prop = max(proportion)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    percentage = paste0(round(proportion*100, 2), "%"),
    label = paste(discipline, percentage, sep = "\n"),
    country = reorder(country, -country_max_prop)
    )

worst_treemap <- ggplot(worst_events, aes(area=country_dis_count, fill=proportion, label=label)) +
  geom_treemap(color="black", linewidth=0.1) +
  facet_wrap(~country) +
  geom_treemap_text(color="gray32", place="center") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low="#F4A6A1", high="#DB4235") +
  labs(title="Underperforming countries show mixed discipline diversity.", 
       subtitle="India, Mexico, Turkey, and the U.S. fail to deliver and also fail to diversify.", 
       caption="Source: Petro Ivaniuk via Olympics.com | By: Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=9, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title = element_text(size=9),
    legend.text = element_text(size=8),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

worst_treemap









Analyzing underperforming teams is tricky, because since they earned fewer medals than they were expected to, the number of medals is significantly smaller than those countries who overperformed. Though, underperforming countries can still be analyzed for their breadth. India relied heavily on Shooting and Mexico on Diving, though both countries medaled in at least four separate disciplines. Turkey medaled in five disciplines, but relied on Boxing pretty heavily. The United States, who took home the most medals in all of the games, relied heavily on Track and Field and Swimming, but also medaled in a variety of other disciplines. Lastly, Argentina was able to medal three times in three very separate disciplines, demonstrating impeccable breadth.
So, while underperforming countries did rely on certain disciplines more than overperforming countries did, the results are rather mixed and inconclusive. We can make some hypotheses on breadth, like more breadth is better, but overall there doesn’t appear to be a clear trend or pattern here.
While breadth may not be a good indicator of why certain countries exceeded expectations and others failed to reach their potential, using GDP is a rather accurate way to discover the countries who underperformed and overperformed at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games.
When all is said and done, GDP ends up accounting for about 75% of the variation in country medal earnings. This is pretty significant. This means that we can use a country’s GDP to predict how many medals they will earn at future Olympic Games reasonably well using just each country’s GDP. Now, as we saw, countries can underperform and overperform, so this prediction is not always accurate. However, we can definitely say that GDP has a relationship with how many medals each country earns.
We also saw an interesting pattern. In this analysis, some high-population countries tended to underperform. This was the case for the U.S., India, and Pakistan. All of these countries had the highest populations in their respective GDP groups and all emerged as some of the worst underperformers. So, while higher GDP indicates higher medal output, it seems that higher population may not have the same effect. This could be due to overcrowding and the need to allocate resources elsewhere, or it could be an extraneous factor. Either way, it is interesting that countries with higher populations ended up underperforming relative to their GDP expectations.
Overall, using GDP to discover which countries underperformed and overperformed at the Paris Games revealed India and the United States as the biggest losers, earning fewer medals than expected, and France and Great Britain as the biggest winners, earning significantly more medals than expected.

title: SEC Women’s Gymnastics Home Judging Bias is Overstated
text:
Gymnastics fans have long speculated about “home bias” in judging. According to the gymternet (the mass of gymnastics enthusiasts on social media), judges are noticeably more lax and often award “gifts,” or scores that are higher than deserved, to the home team. For instance, a step on the landing in a routine is almost always an automatic 0.1 deduction. A routine with only one step and no other errors should score around a 9.85 or a 9.9, but at home, that routine might score a 9.95, which is an impossible score.
The question then becomes: do gymnasts actually score better when competing at home, and if so, which teams benefit most?
To understand whether bias could exist, it helps to start with the most dominant conference in college gymnastics, the SEC. Because of its position in the sport, this conference draws intense scrutiny, with fans regularly criticizing it for rampant home judging bias.
A look at the rankings confirms the SEC’s dominance. Rankings start at week 2 for college gymnastics, the first week of the season in which every team has competed. Since week 2, at least seven of the nine SEC teams have been in the top 10 in all of women’s college gymnastics, demonstrating their powerful command of the sport. If home judging bias exists, we would certainly expect it to show up in the SEC.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(ggbeeswarm)
library(ggbump)
library(ggalt)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)

sec_reg <- read_csv("data/sec_reg_season.csv")
rankings <- read_csv("data/rtn_rankings.csv")

sec_away <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Location == "A") |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(VAULT, BARS, BEAM, FLOOR),
    names_to = "Event",
    values_to = "Score"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Score), Gymnast=="Team Totals") |>
  mutate(Event = factor(Event, levels=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR")))
  
sec_home <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Location == "H") |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(VAULT, BARS, BEAM, FLOOR),
    names_to = "Event",
    values_to = "Score"
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Score), Gymnast=="Team Totals") |>
  mutate(Event = factor(Event, levels=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR")))

sec <- c("Georgia", "LSU", "Florida", "Kentucky", "Missouri", "Alabama", "Arkansas", "Auburn", "Oklahoma")

rankings <- rankings |>
  filter(Week >= 2) |>
  mutate(Rank = as.numeric(Rank)) |>
  filter(Rank < 11)

sec_ranks <- rankings |>
  filter(
    Team %in% sec
  )

sec10_ranks <- rankings |>
  filter(
    Team %in% sec,
    Rank <= 10
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bump(data=rankings |> group_by(Team) |> filter(n() > 1) |> ungroup(), aes(x=Week, y=Rank, group=Team, color="Non-SEC")) + 
  geom_bump(data=sec_ranks |> group_by(Team) |> filter(n() > 1) |> ungroup(), aes(x=Week, y=Rank, group=Team, color="SEC")) +
  geom_point(data=rankings, aes(x=Week, y=Rank, color="Non-SEC"), size = 2) + 
  geom_point(data=sec_ranks, aes(x=Week, y=Rank, color="SEC"), size = 2) +
  geom_text(data = rankings |> filter(Week == min(Week)), aes(x = Week - .2, y=Rank, label = Team), size = 3, hjust = 1) +
  geom_text(data = rankings |> filter(Week == max(Week)), aes(x = Week + .2, y=Rank, label = Team), size = 3, hjust = 0) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Conference", values=c("SEC"="blue", "Non-SEC"="lightgrey"), breaks=c("SEC", "Non-SEC")) +
  scale_y_reverse(limits=c(1, 10), breaks=c(1:10)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(1, 13.5), breaks=c(2:12)) +
  labs(title="The SEC Controls Women's College Gymnastics.", 
       subtitle="Since week 2, nearly every top 10 team has been from the SEC, leaving little room for outsiders.", 
       y= "Rank", 
       x = "Week",
    caption="Source: Road to Nationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.75, "cm"),
    legend.title = element_text(size=9),
    legend.text = element_text(size=8),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )









Most scores in college gymnastics fall between 9.7 and 10.0, a very small range, making differences hard to detect at first glance. However, when home and away event scores are compared, it becomes evident that each event has a higher average at home than it does away, especially on floor.


Code
away_dist <- ggplot(data = sec_away, aes(x = Event, y = Score)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "seashell2") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 3
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(48.5, 50.05), breaks=seq(48.5, 50.0, by=0.25)) +
  labs(subtitle="Away") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face="bold")
  )

home_dist <- ggplot(data = sec_home, aes(x = Event, y = Score, group = Event)) +
  geom_beeswarm(color = "seashell2") +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "errorbar", aes(ymax = after_stat(y), ymin = after_stat(y)),
    width = .75, color = "red", linewidth = 1
  )  +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean, geom = "text", aes(label = sprintf("%.3f", after_stat(y))),
    vjust = -0.75, color="black", size = 3
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits=c(48.5, 50.05), breaks=seq(48.5, 50, by=0.25)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=2.85, xmax=4.6, ymin=49.87, ymax=50.05, fill="white", color="grey", alpha=0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 3.75, y = 49.97, label="Red line denotes average \nteam score per event", size=2.5, color="red") +
  annotate("rect", fill = "blue", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 3.5, xmax = 4.5,
        ymin = 49.43, ymax = 49.62) +
  labs(subtitle="Home") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face="plain")
  )

#to test the significance of floor
t_sec_floor <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "FLOOR")

floor_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_floor)

t_sec_bars <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "BARS")

bars_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_bars)

t_sec_beam <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "BEAM")

beam_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_beam)

t_sec_vault <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01) |>
  pivot_longer(cols=c("VAULT", "BARS", "BEAM", "FLOOR"), names_to = "Event", values_to="Score") |>
  filter(Event == "VAULT")

vault_t <- t.test(Score ~ Location, data=t_sec_vault)

score_distribution <- away_dist + home_dist +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Home Judging Bias Emerges Most Clearly on Floor",
    subtitle = "All the averages show scores for teams to be higher when scored at home than away, \nbut scores for the last event are noticeably higher when teams are at home.",
    caption = "Source: Virtius and Road to Nationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
  )  & 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

score_distribution









Home floor scores are almost 1.5 points higher than away floor scores. In gymnastics, this difference is huge. A standard statistical test confirmed that the floor scoring gap between home and away meets is not due to random chance. It is a real, meaningful difference. No other event showed significance, so the differences between home and away meets on the other events could be due to random chance. If the difference in home and away scoring were simply due to teams comfortable at home, this home scoring bump would show up significantly in the other events too.
Floor being the only significant event does make some sense in context. The home team typically competes floor last, and scores tend to loosen as competitions progress, conceivably benefiting the home team in the final event. So, higher scores are potentially awarded for routines that would have scored lower earlier in the competition.
Additionally, if bias is playing a role, judges may be trying to overcompensate in the last event to ensure the home team wins. Neither explanation proves intentional bias, but both offer plausible reasons why the large gap shows up on floor and nowhere else.
Floor tells part of the story, but the bigger picture lies in total team scores. If home judging bias exists, it should impact entire meets, not just one rotation. Do certain teams in the SEC experience more of a boost at home than others?
To answer that, team scoring averages at home and away were compared across the SEC. The gap between those two numbers reveals which programs gain the most from competing at home.


Code
sec_ha <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals") |>
  group_by(Team, Location) |>
  summarise(
    team_avg = mean(AA)
  )

sec_ha_dumb <- sec_ha |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = Location, values_from = team_avg) |>
  mutate(diff = (H-A))

t_sec_team <- sec_reg |>
  filter(Gymnast == "Team Totals", AA >=50.01)

ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), yend=Team, x=A, xend=H), color="grey", linewidth = 1.5
  )  + 
  geom_point(
    data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), x=A, color="A"), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), x=H, color="H"), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_text(data=sec_ha_dumb, aes(y=reorder(Team, H), x=(H+A)/2, label=round(diff,3)), vjust=-0.8, size=3) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=197.74, xmax=198.25, ymin=1, ymax=2, fill="lightgrey", color="grey", alpha=0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = 198, y = 1.5, label="Values represent home \nminus away averages", size=3, color="black") +
  scale_color_manual(name="Location", values=c("A"="blue", "H"="purple"), labels=c("A"="Away", "H"="Home")) +
  labs(title="All SEC Teams Benefit from Home Judging Advantage", 
       subtitle="Oklahoma, LSU, and Florida see a stronger boost, though all teams score higher at home.", 
       y= "Team", 
       x = "Scores",
    caption="Source: Virtius and Road to Nationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
    ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, face = "plain"),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size=8, face = "plain"),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )









Top SEC teams receive noticeably higher scores at home than when away, whereas teams in the middle or bottom of the conference (with the exception of Auburn) see minimal differences.
Fans often criticize Oklahoma, LSU, and Florida for egregious home scoring, claiming those teams are being wildly overscored. It is important to keep in mind that these are also the top three teams in all of NCAA women’s gymnastics, so they have the most eyes on them. Also, those at the top often receive the most hate. Fans like the underdog and get frustrated when the top teams continue to perform well. So, while fans complain about these teams most, it doesn’t necessarily mean that their higher scores at home are actually due to a judging bias.
Using a standard statistical cutoff, where any number below -2 is considered significant, we can test teams to see if the difference between their home and away averages is due to random chance or could actually be representative of home judging bias. Though the data for Oklahoma, LSU, and Florida do demonstrate differences in average, when tested for significance, it is revealed that only one team in the SEC experiences a meaningful bump in scores at home, Oklahoma. Competing at home meaningfully inflates the Sooner’s scores, showing signs that they experience home judging bias.
For all other SEC teams, the differences in home and away scores exist but they are not significant, and we cannot say that home judging affects how well those teams do.


Code
teams <- c("Florida", "LSU", "Oklahoma", "Auburn", "Georgia", "Arkansas", "Missouri", "Kentucky", "Alabama")
team_t_statistic <- numeric(length(teams))

for (i in 1:length(teams)) {
  team_data <- t_sec_team |>
    filter(Team == teams[i])
  
  team_t_statistic[i] <- t.test(AA ~ Location, data=team_data)$statistic
}

team_t_tests <- data.frame(teams, team_t_statistic) |>
  arrange(team_t_statistic)

team_t_tests |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    teams = "Team",
    team_t_statistic = "Statistical Cutoff"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Only Oklahoma's Home Scores Demonstrate Bias",
    subtitle = "While most SEC teams see insignificant differences, the Sooners gain a measurable advantage at home."
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Source: Virtius and Road to Nationals | By: Cassie Nesheim"
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "The t-test statistic is the statistical cutoff used.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = team_t_statistic)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "indianred1"),
      cell_text(color = "black")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = team_t_statistic >= 2 | team_t_statistic <= -2)
  )








Only Oklahoma's Home Scores Demonstrate Bias


While most SEC teams see insignificant differences, the Sooners gain a measurable advantage at home.


Team
Statistical Cutoff1




Oklahoma
-2.847534437


Auburn
-1.791664031


LSU
-1.209713471


Florida
-0.911681089


Arkansas
-0.841117949


Georgia
-0.651607535


Missouri
-0.336123599


Alabama
-0.128411112


Kentucky
-0.007233853



1 The t-test statistic is the statistical cutoff used.


Source: Virtius and Road to Nationals | By: Cassie Nesheim








Home scores across the SEC are, on average, higher than those on the road, especially on floor. But when those differences are tested more closely and each team is examined, the story becomes less dramatic. Only Oklahoma shows a statistically meaningful home advantage, while the rest of the conference does not.
In a conference where dominance is the standard, home judging bias should be easy to detect. Instead, it largely fails to appear.
While fans may continue to debate home bias and argue the home team is being “gifted” scores, aside from the Sooners, their claims have no merit. If home judging bias were widespread, it would likely show up clearly in the top teams, like those in the SEC, but the data suggests otherwise. So, at least for now, gymnastics fans must come to terms with the fact that home scores are slightly higher, with the reassurance that it is not so sizeable that the results of each meet change depending on who is at home.
Home judging bias may exist in isolated cases, but it is far from having the dramatic effect it is often claimed to have on SEC teams.
